Tafseer:
The eloquence of the interrogative style:
'Amma' is originally 'amma', but in common usage, just as the sound of certain letters is suppressed, the 'alif' has been dropped from 'amma', and it is used in this manner.
The mockery of the deniers of the Resurrection: 'Tasa'ul' means to ask and question one another about something. Questioning can be for seeking information and research, and occasionally, solely for vain talk and mockery. Here, it is used in the sense of mockery. The Qur'an states in various places that when the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) recited the Surahs of warning to the Quraysh, the warmth of the tone, the majesty of the style, and the certainty of the proofs quickly spread the talk in every circle. To protect their common people from its influence, the Quraysh, along with other foolish plans, adopted the cheap tactic of making it the subject of joke and sport in their assemblies, trying to convey that this was not worthy of any serious consideration but was merely a fantasy that should not affect anyone. Some asked: 'How is it possible that when people turn to dust, they will be resurrected!' Others added: 'Will our ancestors also be resurrected, who turned to dust long ago and whose graves have no trace left!' A third affirmed with force: 'Impossible, impossible, this can never happen; these are all merely stories.'
They would mock Hell and its Fire, saying: 'How excellent will be the Fire that contains water as well as trees!' and another would praise him for this point, asking if such things could be comprehended by intellect.
When the Qur'an informed them that nineteen keepers are appointed over Hell, they made this the subject of their sport. One said: 'If there are only nineteen, I am sufficient to deal with this many on my own.' Another boasted: 'Then there is no fear; I am not weak to deal with the rest!'
In short, whatever they were told regarding the Resurrection and its conditions, instead of learning a lesson, they tried to mock it to prevent their common people from being affected. This is described here as 'tasa'ul', asking in a highly severe tone what they are questioning one another about.
Beginning this Surah with this question reveals its temperament—showing that they will be told that what they are mocking is not a subject of joke and sport, but if they reflect, it is a subject for beating their heads and weeping tears of blood.

A style of the language:
'Naba'' refers to a great event or important news. Although the interrogative particle is not explicitly mentioned before this verse, grammatically it falls under the same interrogative mentioned first. A highly clear example of this exists in Surah Al-Inshirah, where He states:
(Did We not expand for you your breast? And We removed from you your burden) (Al-Inshirah, 94:1-2).
Note that the phrase 'And We removed from you your burden' is not explicitly under the interrogative, but is in a simple declarative style, yet grammatically it falls under it. Examples of this are numerous in the Qur'an, and other examples will appear in this Surah as well. Commentators are generally unfamiliar with this style; therefore, they translate such expressive sentences in a declarative style, which does not convey the actual force of the discourse, as there is a vast difference between expression and declaration.
The meaning of the verse is: Are these people mocking with such audacity the great news of the Resurrection and the Day of Recompense that is being communicated to them? This news is such that its concern should have deprived them of the pleasure of sleep and food, but they are so unfortunate that instead of fearing it and preparing for it, they have made it the subject of their mockery.

The intellectual contradiction of the deniers of the Resurrection:
'That over which they are in disagreement' (alladhi hum feehi mukhtalifun) - 'Ikhtilaf' (disagreement) manifests two meanings simultaneously: difference of opinion and contradiction of thought, and both are interconnected, as difference of opinion arises from contradiction of thought. We have repeatedly explained that regarding the Resurrection, the polytheists of the Arabs suffered from an extremely severe contradiction of thought. (Footnote 1) One group among them openly denied it, while another group, which was larger, instead of open denial, raised various doubts regarding it. Their assumption was that first, its occurrence is highly unlikely, and even if it occurred, there was no need to worry. They believed their return would be to their deities, who would save them from God's grasp. They also suffered from the delusion: 'Who can know the deeds and words of such countless creations to account for them?' They also believed: 'Since our state is good in this world, which is a proof that we are approved by God, there is no reason why He should deprive us of this honor in the Resurrection.'
Alongside these false beliefs, they also confessed many correct beliefs that refuted these false ones, but accepting the Resurrection was contrary to their desires; therefore, they were not ready to review and resolve their contradiction of thought despite the repeated reminders of the Qur'an. Yet, it is a natural requirement of intellect that if a person lacks certainty in a matter on which his eternal success or ruin depends, he should listen to those who are pointing to his contradiction of thought, so that he may be saved from ruin. This is his own need, not the need of those who remind him. The Qur'an draws attention to the reality that their contradiction of thought and uncertainty regarding the great event the Qur'an is informing them of is in no way good for them. This is a matter of eternal ruin or success. The Qur'an has done them a great favor by showing them the path to emerge from this contradiction, and they should have appreciated it, but in their misfortune, they have made it a subject of sport.
_____
Footnote 1: Refer to the commentary of Surah An-Naml, verse 66.

Warning to the deniers:
This is a warning in highly powerful words: the sweet dreams they are dreaming will never be realized. The end of which the Qur'an is warning them will surely face them. The repetition of the sentence is not merely to confirm the claim, but to state the reality. Allah's messengers warned their nations of two punishments simultaneously: first, the punishment that has always befallen a nation when it rejected its messenger; second, the punishment they will face on the Day of Resurrection. Keeping both of these punishments in view, this warning is repeated twice.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 4 and 5 was combined in the original text.]

Arguing from the signs of providence:
In these verses, Allah Almighty has drawn attention to the signs in this universe that serve as clear proofs of His power, wisdom, mercy, providence, monotheism, the Resurrection, and the necessity of a Day of Recompense. Finally, the conclusion is presented: (Indeed, the Day of Judgement is an appointed time) (verse 17)—showing that whoever reflects on these signs will be forced to confess that a Day of Decision must inevitably come, and its time is fixed with the Creator.
'Have We not made the earth a resting place and the mountains as pegs?' (alam naj'ali-l-arda mihada, wa-l-jibala awtada) - First, attention is drawn to the earth and its mountains: if man denies the Day of Recompense, does he not reflect on the arrangement of providence Allah has made for him without any right on his part—making the earth a resting place for his peace, and fixing mountains in it as pegs to keep it stable, preventing any shaking.
The various wisdoms behind fixing mountains in the earth are pointed to in the Qur'an. In the previous Surah, one of its great benefits was mentioned. Elsewhere, He explained its wisdom as: (And He cast into the earth firmly fixed mountains, lest it should sway with you) (Luqman, 31:10).
Here, it is pointed out: if man reflects, he will easily understand that the Lord Who is nurturing him with such care in this resting place of the earth, how can He not bring a Day to reward those who recognized His providence and completed their duties, and to punish those who chose ungratefulness and disobedience. Providence is bound to accountability. If it were not so, it would mean that to the Creator, the grateful and the rebellious are equal, which is contrary to His noble attributes.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 6 and 7 was combined in the original text.]

'And We created you in pairs' (wa khalaqnakum azwaja) - Although the style of the text has become declarative, grammatically it is connected to: 'Have We not made the earth a resting place'. This was explained under verses 1-2.
This points to the greatest source of comfort Allah has provided for man in this world. It is stated: We did not create you alone, but created your pair from your own kind so that she might be a source of peace and comfort for you. Note that Allah has created everything in pairs in this world, and these pairs share such a deep relationship that neither can fulfill the purpose of creation alone. Although apparently they are opposites, His power has placed within them such physical and spiritual inclinations that they find peace and comfort only in living together, fulfilling a noble purpose. This characteristic is shared between husband and wife, as stated in Surah Ar-Rum, verse 21:
(He created for you from yourselves mates that you may find tranquility in them; and He placed between you affection and mercy) (30:21).
This harmony among opposites is presented in the Qur'an as a proof of monotheism and the Resurrection, and we have explained it.

'And made your sleep [a means for] rest' (wa ja'alna nawmakum subata) - 'Sabt' and 'subat' literally mean cutting off, but here the term means rest and comfort. Sleep is described as 'subat' because it cuts off the continuity of work, providing an opportunity to recover from fatigue and refresh the strength of the body.

'And made the night as a clothing' (wa ja'alna-l-layla libasa) - We made the night as a clothing for you. The night being a clothing means that just as clothing covers a person and provides comfort, the sheet of night covers him, protecting him from distractions so he can rest and prepare to enter the field of work again.

'And made the day for livelihood' (wa ja'alna-n-nahara ma'asha) - We made the day the time for seeking livelihood.
Drawing attention to these signs is intended to show: whoever reflects on them will reach the conclusion that this night and day do not revolve on their own, nor is their revolution without purpose; rather, a Wise and All-Powerful Lord is keeping them busy in His service so that people benefit from them and remain grateful to their Lord Who made this arrangement for their livelihood, comfort, and ease. At the same time, they should remember that this arrangement of providence requires a Day when He sees who lived with open eyes and who remained blind, and deals with them accordingly.

'And constructed above you seven strong [heavens]' (wa banayna fawqakum sab'an shidada) - After the signs of the earth, attention is drawn to the heaven. It is stated: look above, We constructed above you seven strong heavens. Although the word 'heaven' is not explicitly mentioned, the attributes indicate it. 'Shidad' (strong/mighty) is identical to what is stated in Surah Al-Mulk:
(He Who created seven heavens in layers. You do not see in the creation of the Most Merciful any inconsistency. So return [your] vision; do you see any rifts? Then return [your] vision twice; [your] vision will return to you humbled while it is fatigued.) (Al-Mulk, 67:3-4).
The meaning is: wherever you look at this endless ceiling, you will find it solid and completely free of any defect; you cannot point to any minor flaw in it.

'And made [therein] a burning lamp' (wa ja'alna sirajan wahhaja) - And We placed a burning lamp in the heaven. This obviously refers to the sun, which is the source of light, heat, and life-giving power in this world. This also reveals that different deities do not rule in the heavens and earth, but both are subject to the same All-Powerful and Eternal God; otherwise, how could this harmony exist, such that the sun of the heaven serves the inhabitants of the earth in this manner.

'And sent down from the rain clouds pouring water' (wa anzalna minal-mu'sirati ma'an thajjaja) - 'Mu'sirat' is used as an attribute of rain clouds, referring to clouds filled with water or those that squeeze out water. Both meanings are close.
'Ma'un thajjaj' refers to heavy, abundant, and pouring rain.
The Qur'an has used rain to argue for all of its fundamental claims. Although the harmony between heaven and earth is a proof of monotheism, the aspect of providence is prominent here, serving as a primary proof of accountability and recompense.

'That We may bring forth thereby grain and vegetation and gardens of thick growth' (li-nukhrija bihi habban-wa nabatan-wa jannatin alfafa) - It is stated: We send down this rain from the heavens to bring forth grain for you, grass for your cattle, and gardens of thick growth.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 15 and 16 was combined in the original text.]

'Indeed, the Day of Judgement is an appointed time' (inna yawma-l-fasli kana meeqata) - This summarizes the entire preceding argument: this arrangement of providence and nourishment from the heaven to the earth bears witness that the Lord Who did all of this will not leave man unaccountable; rather, He has appointed a Day of Decision when He will gather everyone and judge who recognized His providence and completed their duties. Then, He will reward or punish each according to their deeds.
This is stated as if the Day of Decision is proclaiming its own arrival within the system of this universe. Unfortunate are those who do not hear it. Sadi wrote:
'The clouds, wind, moon, sun, and heaven are busy,
so that you may obtain your bread and not consume it in heedlessness.'

Depiction of the terror of the Resurrection:
After explaining the proofs of the Day of Decision, these verses portray the upheaval that will occur in the universe, and the end that the rebels will face.
'The Day the Trumpet is blown and you will come in multitudes' (yawma yunfakhu fi-s-soori fa-ta'toona afwaja) - It is stated: when Allah Almighty gathers you for this Day of Decision, He will face no difficulty. A single blast will be blown into the Trumpet, and you will emerge from your graves in multitudes, hastening toward the Caller. Elsewhere, He states that they will emerge like scattered locusts, hastening toward the Caller without any deviation.

'And the heaven is opened and will become gateways' (wa futihati-s-sama'u fa-kanat abwaba) - And this heaven, which appears solid and firm today, will split open on that Day, appearing as if it consists of gateways everywhere.

'And the mountains are removed and will be [as] a mirage' (wa suyyirati-l-jibalu fa-kanat saraba) - And these mountains, which are firmly fixed today, will be uprooted and blown away, and while they are solid rocks today, they will become like pouring sand dunes.

'Indeed, Hell has been lying in wait, for the transgressors, a place of return' (inna jahannama kanat mirsada, li-t-tagheena ma'aba) - Following this upheaval, Hell will appear suddenly, as if it was lying in wait under the cover of this upheaval to become the place of return for the transgressors. No preparation will be needed, nor will any respite be granted to the transgressors.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 21 and 22 was combined in the original text.]

'In which they will remain for ages' (labitheena feeha ahqaba) - 'Ahqab' means ages. This was explained elsewhere by: (abiding therein eternally) (khalideena feeha abada)—meaning they will abide in it forever. Some have used this to argue that Hell will end one day, but this is incorrect. The correct way of understanding is to interpret the general in the light of the specific, not the specific in the light of the general. 'Abiding therein eternally' is specific, and 'ahqab' is general. We will interpret this general in the light of the specific, not the other way around.
Furthermore, this describes the end of the rebels and transgressors, regarding whom the Qur'an explicitly states that they will never emerge from Hell.

'They will not taste therein [any] coolness or drink except scalding water and purulence' (la yadhooqoona feeha bardaw-wa-la sharaba, illa hameemaw-wa ghassaqa) - In this Hell, they will taste neither coolness nor any drink except boiling water and purulence. 'Ghassaq' has been interpreted as pus and blood, and also as cold, foul water. Coolness is completely negated. In Surah Al-Mursalat, the shadow ('dhill') of smoke is described as: neither cool nor beneficial against the flames.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 24 and 25 was combined in the original text.]

'An appropriate recompense' (jaza'aw-wifaqan) - That is: what they receive will be the exact and full recompense for their deeds. The earnings they made in the world will face them. In the Hereafter, every virtue and evil will bear its fruit, which will face the human.

'Indeed, they were not expecting an account and denied Our signs with [emphatic] denial' (innahum kanu la yarjoona hisaba, wa kadhdhabu bi-ayatina kidhdhaba) - That is: they had no fear of any account; therefore, they remained untroubled, and rejected Our signs—which warned of this Day—with defiance. 'Kidhdhaba' is a verbal noun used for emphasis. Although its pattern is different, its meaning is identical to 'taktheeb' (denial). The meaning is: they rejected Our signs with complete defiance.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 27 and 28 was combined in the original text.]

'But all things We have enumerated in writing' (wa kulla shay'in ahsaynahu kitaba) - That is: they assumed there is neither account nor punishment, but We kept a written record of their every action. A written record is a proof of complete care, showing that no oversight can occur.

'So taste [the penalty], and never will We increase you except in punishment' (fa-dhooqoo fa-lan-nazeedakum illa 'adhaba) - The style is changed to the second person to make the presentation of their end more powerful. It is stated: you remained untroubled, so taste the consequence of your deeds. Along with this, to make them completely despair of the future, He stated: We will never increase you except in punishment. No decrease can be expected. Whatever change occurs will only increase the punishment.

The reward of those who fear God:
Following the end of the rebels, this describes the reward of the righteous (muttaqeen) to show the other side of the picture. It is stated: indeed, success belongs to those who feared the Day of Recompense. It should noted that what keeps life stable on the straight path is the fear of the Hereafter. Whoever possesses this is righteous, and whose heart is devoid of it, Satan makes his home in him and he commits disobedience without any fear.

'Gardens and grapevines' (hada'iqa wa a'naba) - This details their success: they will have gardens and grapes. 'Hada'iq' refers specifically to palm gardens, but the finest manner of palm gardens was to have rows of palm trees along the boundaries and grapevines and other fruits in the middle. Mentioning 'grapeveins' after 'gardens' is a specific mention after a general mention.

'And full-bosomed [companions] of equal age' (wa kawa'iba atraba) - This refers to the companions. Describing them, it is stated: they will be of youthful age and equal in age. Equality in age is necessary for mutual comfort, interest, and shared tastes.

'And a full cup. No vain talk will they hear therein or any falsehood' (wa ka'sand-dihaqa, la yasma'oona feeha laghwaw-wa-la kidhdhaba) - That is: they will have full cups of pure drink, but this drink will be free from the vain talk and falsehood that are associated with the wine of this world. It will be superior in its pleasure, but will not affect their intellect, causing them to speak incoherently or falsely. It should noted that in their intoxication, drinkers occasionally utter baseless accusations that cause permanent enmity between families and tribes. In societies where honor is lost, people ignore such talk, but the Arabs were highly sensitive. If someone uttered a word affecting another's honor in his intoxication, it would lead to long-lasting consequences. The word 'kidhdhab' points to such statements.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 34 and 35 was combined in the original text.]

'As reward from your Lord, a gift [according to] account' (jaza'am-mir-rabbika 'ata'an hisaba) - Just as He stated regarding the disbelievers: 'An appropriate recompense'—showing they will receive a punishment exactly matching their deeds—He stated regarding the people of Paradise that they will receive their reward in accordance with a full account. Allah Almighty will not let even their smallest virtue go in vain, and the promise of His grace is in addition to this.

It is stated: for the believers, this reward will be from the Most Merciful God, Who is the Lord of the heavens and earth and whatever is between them, and no one is His partner in anything. 
Nullifying their assumed intercession: 'They will possess no authority to address Him' (la yamlikoona minhu khitaba). This points to the disbelievers and their assumed deities—showing that they assume their deities have access to God and can say whatever they wish and have it accepted. This is completely false. No one will have the authority to address Him; only those permitted by Him will speak, and they will speak only the truth.

The polytheists relied heavily on the intercession of the angels, whom they worshipped as God's daughters. It is stated: on that Day, Gabriel and the other angels will stand in rows before the Lord of Majesty as servants. None of them will speak except those permitted by the Most Merciful, and they will speak only what is correct. That is: if the polytheists assume their deities can Naz-o-Tadallul (coax) and intercede for them as they wish, it is their vain hope.
'The Spirit' (Ar-Rooh) refers to Gabriel, who is the leader of all angels, which is why he is mentioned first—showing that when Gabriel's state is such, what of others! Some have interpreted it as human souls, but there is no context for this.

This is a declaration of communication: warning people of this Day was necessary, and it has been done. Now, the responsibility lies on the people themselves. It is stated: the Day of whose arrival you are warned is certain. It is bound to happen. No one can prevent it, nor will anyone help another on that Day; so whoever wishes may choose the path to his Lord.
'So whoever wills may take to his Lord a way' points first to the reality: in this matter, the responsibility of Allah and the Messenger is only to communicate the warning, not to force it upon their hearts.
Second, it shows: on that Day, refuge belongs solely to Allah Almighty, and no other refuge will be available.
Third, it shows: the way to make Allah your refuge is to choose His path in this world. Whoever fails to do so here cannot find His refuge in the Hereafter.

This is the final warning. It is stated: We have warned you of a near punishment—showing that deciding the fate of a nation after the Messenger's warning is certain, and this punishment will be the precursor to the punishment of the Resurrection. Furthermore, regarding the Resurrection as distant is foolishness because this life is short, and whoever dies faces his Resurrection: 'Whoever dies, his Resurrection has occurred.'
'And the disbeliever will say, "Oh, I wish that I were dust!"' (wa yaqoolu-l-kafiru ya laytanee kuntu turaba) - That is: on that Day, everyone's deeds will face them, and those who made no preparation will beat their heads in misery, wishing they were dust and had never existed!
